Concept 3: Role conflict: the tension that arises when multiple roles carry contradictory expectations, affecting how one acts.

questions.question: How do shared assumptions—summed up as "the way things get done around here"—influence both how people think and how an organization performs?

options.A: They remove uncertainty by giving explicit rules for every situation so everyone acts the same.
options.B: They work as implicit cognitive lenses that shape how people interpret events, guide decisions and habits, and so affect coordination and performance.
options.C: They are just descriptive labels and do not change people's behavior or outcomes.
options.D: They force a single corporate identity and stop any variation in values or norms across teams.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened to one clear sentence; wording simplified to plain language while keeping the idea that assumptions shape thinking and performance; answer choices made concise and plausible.
concepts_tested: Shared assumptions and patterns: culture is a "pattern of basic assumptions" or "the way things get done around here" that are learned and guide thinking and action.

Culture's influence on behavior and outcomes: culture affects how people interact, how decisions are made, and contributes to organizational performance (e.g., competitive advantage, internal alignment).
Subcultures and organizational identity: cultures can be diverse with subcultures, and identity/values help differentiate and shape stakeholder perceptions and behavior within the organization.

questions.question: How do emergent properties in a complex system appear, and why can't you predict them by looking only at the individual parts?

options.A: They come from linear, additive effects of parts, so the whole is just the sum of its parts.
options.B: They come from nonlinear interactions and network relationships among parts, producing system-level properties that can't be deduced from any single part.
options.C: They appear only when a central controller imposes new rules that override local component behavior.
options.D: They arise because each component acts independently in the same way, so system behavior is fully predictable from individual parts.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened wording to one clear question sentence, used plain language while keeping key terms like 'nonlinear' and 'network', and made all options concise and plausible.
concepts_tested: Emergence: system-level properties arise from interactions among parts and nonlinear relationships.
Feedback loops and adaptation: mechanisms like information feedback and adaptive change drive dynamics and self-organization.
Relational/network perspective: understanding via interactions and networks (and environment) rather than strict reductionism.

questions.question: In the attractor/noise view of self-organization, how do random fluctuations (noise) help a system reach more organized states?

options.A: Noise suppresses dynamics and keeps the system stuck in its initial local attractor.
options.B: Noise makes the system drift randomly, destroying attractors and preventing organized states.
options.C: Noise lets the system stochastically explore different basins of attraction, helping it escape shallow attractors and, through nonlinear feedback, settle into deeper, more stable attractors.
options.D: Noise deterministically selects a single global attractor regardless of initial conditions.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence; kept attractor/noise framework and the role of random fluctuations helping escape shallow attractors and reach deep, stable ones; removed historical and disciplinary details.
concepts_tested: Emergence: global order arising from decentralized, local interactions governed by nonlinear dynamics and feedback.
Four ingredients: strong dynamical non-linearity (with positive/negative feedback), balance of exploitation and exploration, multiple interactions among components, and energy availability.
Attractor/Noise framework: systems move toward attractors in state space, with noise facilitating exploration to reach deep attractors (order from noise).

questions.question: How does using the disaster life cycle (interphase, event, response, recovery) help improve disaster planning and action?

options.A: It makes sure resources are used only during the disaster event.
options.B: It forces a strict order where each phase must finish before the next starts, reducing overlap and flexibility.
options.C: It reveals activities that span phases and creates continuous feedback, letting planners prepare ahead and adapt quickly during the event.
options.D: It shifts attention away from policy and long-term planning toward only immediate clinical care.

questions.simplification_notes: Changed the original longer question into one concise sentence; shortened each option to 1–2 sentences while keeping the core idea that the lifecycle highlights cross-phase work, coordination, and adaptability.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Disaster life cycle as a framework guiding planning, response, and recovery, with distinct phases and cross-phase activities.
Concept 2: The liaison/coordination role of disaster medicine, illustrating the mechanism by which clinical care, emergency management, incident command, and policy interact.
Concept 3: The unique practice scope of disaster medicine (emergency-based practice) and its implications for policy development, planning, and training.

questions.question: In a feedback amplifier, how can the same feedback path both boost the desired signal (regenerative gain) and also cause unwanted hum or noise?

options.A: Feedback reduces the loop gain, eliminating noise while increasing the signal.
options.B: Feedback multiplies both the desired signal and any noise by the loop gain; if the phase lines up it boosts the signal, but it can also amplify hum, lowering the signal-to-noise ratio.
options.C: Feedback only changes how fast the system responds, not its amplitude, so hum must come from outside the feedback path.
options.D: Feedback makes the system nonlinear and thus always increases distortion and worsens signal quality.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question one clear sentence, replaced 'regenerative gain' with 'boost the desired signal', kept key terms like 'loop gain', 'phase', and 'signal-to-noise ratio'; shortened each option to 1–2 sentences.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Feedback creates a closed-loop, where outputs influence future inputs, making simple linear cause-and-effect reasoning insufficient.
Concept 2: Feedback mechanisms act as regulators in physical systems (e.g., float valve, centrifugal governors) to maintain or control a variable (water level, speed).
Concept 3: Feedback can both enhance performance (regenerative amplification) and introduce unwanted effects (hum), illustrating the dual, trade-off nature of feedback in systems.

questions.question: Why do policy blueprints often fail to fully predict what happens "on the ground", showing the need for ongoing evaluation and revision?

options.A: Because real-world contexts are complex: different stakeholders respond in varied ways and feedback loops can create unexpected effects the blueprint did not foresee.
options.B: Because policy blueprints are inherently unreliable and should never be trusted.
options.C: Because evidence-based policy guarantees fixed, predetermined outcomes once it is implemented.
options.D: Because after implementation a policy's effects are totally independent of local context and never need adjustment.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question shorter and clearer for high school students; kept the core idea that complexity, stakeholder responses, and feedback loops cause unpredicted outcomes. Options were made concise and plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Policy as a deliberate mechanism to guide decisions toward rational outcomes (design principle and intended effect)
Concept 2: The relationship and distinction between policy, law, and rules; subjective versus objective decision-making and how policies influence behavior and decisions
Concept 3: The dynamic implementation and evaluation of policy (from blueprints to on-the-ground effects; role of evidence-based policy and the potential for unexpected outcomes)

questions.question: A single compass bearing to a known landmark puts you somewhere along a line on the map. Why do bearings to two different known landmarks let you find one exact position?

options.A: Each bearing is a line of possible positions; two independent bearing lines cross at a single point, giving your position.
options.B: Two bearings cancel measurement error and give an exact position, whereas one bearing cannot remove error.
options.C: A single bearing needs your speed or movement information to fix position; two bearings supply that so you can get a point.
options.D: The second bearing tells how far you've moved along the first bearing, converting the line into a point.

questions.simplification_notes: Wording shortened and made more direct for high school students; kept 'bearing' but explained it as a line of possible positions; wrong answers kept plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Position estimation relative to known references or patterns (e.g., charts, stars, landmarks, GNSS) as the core principle of navigation.
Concept 2: Use of measurements and instruments (compass, astrolabe, charts, GNSS) to convert observations into position and direction for guiding movement.
Concept 3: Domain-specific reference frames and tools (marine charts, land maps/marks, flight progress in aeronautics) and how technological evolution enabled navigation principles.

questions.question: Why is repeating steps with feedback important in the engineering design process?

options.A: To lock in requirements early and avoid any future changes.
options.B: It lets engineers refine the problem, goals, and solutions as new information appears, lowering the risk of a poor fit before committing.
options.C: To ensure the final design is completed in a single phase without testing.
options.D: To minimize stakeholder involvement so the project can move faster.

questions.simplification_notes: Reworded 'iteration with feedback' to 'repeating steps with feedback', shortened sentences, kept the idea that iteration refines understanding, objectives, and solutions and reduces risk; distractors made short and plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The design process is iterative and feedback-driven; cycles repeat as needed before progressing.
Concept 2: Establishing objectives and criteria drives design decisions, synthesis, analysis, testing, and evaluation.
Concept 3: There are multiple, non-linear framings/models of the design process, indicating variability in steps and their overlap.

questions.question: How does knowledge management help organizations learn faster across their boundaries?

options.A: By putting all knowledge in a single repository and assuming it will automatically reach every unit.
options.B: By creating structured information flows, codifying knowledge, and using social methods to turn tacit knowledge into shareable knowledge, which raises absorptive capacity and speeds up learning cycles.
options.C: By focusing on storing archives instead of active sharing, assuming tacit knowledge cannot be transferred.
options.D: By limiting knowledge sharing to protect competitive advantage, which prevents learning across the organization.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, used clearer language while keeping key terms (codification, tacit knowledge, absorptive capacity, learning cycles). Options were made concise and kept plausible distractors.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Knowledge management as a set of processes to create, share, and apply knowledge to support organizational goals, and its role in enabling organizational learning.
Concept 2: The relationship between knowledge management and organizational learning, including how KM serves as a strategic asset and facilitates information sharing that drives OL.

Concept 3: Transorganizational knowledge in supply chains and the challenges it presents, including how Industry 4.0 and digital transformation affect knowledge flows across organizational boundaries.

questions.question: Why do scientists describe planetary boundaries as thresholds or tipping points that can cause abrupt, non-linear changes, instead of assuming effects grow gradually as human pressures increase? This view ties to treating the Holocene as a stable "safe zone" and to evidence showing some boundaries have already been crossed.

options.A: Because Earth system processes have strong feedbacks and interactions that can flip a system into a new state once a critical point is crossed, causing rapid, disproportionate change.
options.B: Because Earth system changes always happen gradually and proportionally, so boundaries are only convenient labels.
options.C: Because every boundary has the same tipping-point value, so all systems behave identically once crossed.
options.D: Because crossing any boundary automatically causes immediate, total collapse of ecosystems in every case.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened to two sentences; replaced 'thresholds' with 'tipping points' and linked the question to the Holocene safe-zone and evidence of transgressed boundaries; kept answer choices concise and plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Thresholds and non-linear responses — planetary boundaries are limits that, when crossed, may trigger abrupt, large-scale environmental changes.
Concept 2: The Holocene as a normative safe zone — a stable regime under which human societies thrived, used to justify the need to stay within boundaries to maintain stability.
Concept 3: Evidence-driven framework with updates — initial boundaries and their statuses (2009) and subsequent updates (2015) show how the model is refined as new data and analyses become available.

questions.question: Why can a small change in cellular pH greatly change an enzyme's activity even when the substrate amount stays the same?

options.A: Because small pH changes alter ionization (gain or loss of H+) of active-site amino acids and sometimes the substrate, changing electrostatic interactions and the active site's shape needed to stabilize the transition state, which changes catalysis.
options.B: Because pH changes induce mutations that alter the protein's amino-acid sequence (primary structure).
options.C: Because enzymes are rigid and do not respond to pH, so any observed change is experimental error.
options.D: Because pH changes only affect water availability, not enzyme–substrate binding or catalysis.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened to one sentence; 'ionization' explained as gain/loss of H+; 'active-site residues' changed to 'active-site amino acids'; kept technical terms like 'transition state'.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Structure–function relationships of biological macromolecules (proteins, nucleic acids, carbohydrates, lipids) underpin cellular processes.
Concept 2: Metabolism as the network of chemical reactions that enables cells to harvest and use energy.
Concept 3: Biochemistry as an interdisciplinary field connecting chemistry, biology, and applied areas (medicine, nutrition, agriculture, biotechnology).

questions.question: Why don't ISO 14040/14044 rules make all LCAs give the same results, and what should you do when comparing different LCA studies?

options.A: The standards set a common framework but allow choices on system boundaries, allocation methods, data sources, and impact categories; different choices can change results, so you must check each study's documented decisions before comparing them.
options.B: The standards prescribe exact data values and fixed boundaries, so identical results should occur if everyone follows them exactly.
options.C: ISO always requires a cradle-to-grave scope and forbids including the use phase, so differences only come from missing use-phase data.
options.D: The standards supply standard datasets that remove uncertainty, so any remaining differences mean a study did not comply with ISO.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened to two sentences; technical terms like 'system boundaries' and 'allocation' kept but made concise; options rewritten as short, clear statements while preserving the original correct answer.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Cradle-to-grave (life-cycle) framing as the holistic basis for assessing environmental impacts across all stages of a product’s life.
Concept 2: Inventory and emissions linkage to cumulative environmental impact and its use in comparing carbon footprints.
Concept 3: Standardization through ISO 14040/14044 and the associated boundaries/criticisms, highlighting how guidelines shape but do not perfectly constrain outcomes.

questions.question: Why does choosing a research methodology change what conclusions you can reasonably draw from the same dataset?

options.A: Because some methods are faster and less thorough, so they give weaker or less reliable conclusions.
options.B: Because different methodologies carry different assumptions about what counts as evidence, how data connect to claims, and what generalizations are allowed, so the same data can support different defensible conclusions under different frameworks.
options.C: Because data are always objective and unambiguous; using standard formulas means the method does not affect interpretation.
options.D: Because the dataset contains multiple equally true answers, and the chosen method just picks one of them.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, used simpler phrasing, and made each option concise while keeping the core idea that methods include assumptions about evidence and inference.
concepts_tested: The relationship between method choice and the conclusions/claims that can be drawn from data (how different methods can lead to different interpretations).
The distinction and integration of quantitative and qualitative methodologies, including the value and rationale for mixed-methods.
The role of philosophical background assumptions in determining what counts as evidence and how methods are evaluated.

questions.question: Digital distribution makes the marginal cost of delivering one more song to a listener nearly zero. How does this change the music industry's economics and strategy, and why does it both help creators and create new competitive pressures?

options.A: It lowers marginal cost so pay-per-stream and data-driven engagement become central; this helps independent artists reach listeners but raises competition and reliance on big digital platforms.
options.B: It increases marginal cost of distribution, which reduces the threat from piracy.
options.C: It makes direct-to-consumer sales the only viable channel and removes the need for record labels.
options.D: It guarantees piracy will always outperform legal channels, ending legitimate music sales.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened to two clear sentences; removed detailed history and jargon; options made concise but plausible; preserved the core idea about near-zero marginal cost, platform-driven revenue, indie opportunities, and increased competition.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The shift from sheet music to recorded music as the primary product transformed the structure and business model of the modern music industry.
Concept 2: Market concentration and power dynamics (a few major labels and a dominant live/promotions ecosystem) shape who controls distribution, revenue, and access to markets.

Concept 3: Digital distribution (including legal stores and piracy) disrupted traditional monetization and distribution channels, creating new opportunities and challenges for revenue and competition.

questions.question: Why do futures studies explore several plausible futures instead of predicting just one?

options.A: To increase forecasting accuracy by finding a single most likely outcome.
options.B: To focus on historical trends and extrapolate the only possible path forward.
options.C: To widen decision-makers' thinking to include different possible paths so strategies remain useful under many conditions.
options.D: To avoid making value judgments and keep forecasts purely descriptive.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question and answers shorter and clearer for high school readers, removed extra historical and disciplinary details, and kept the key idea that plural futures improve planning robustness.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Emphasis on exploring multiple plausible futures (plural futures) rather than sole prediction, as a core methodological principle.
Concept 2: Pattern-based analysis of sources, patterns, and causes of change and stability to develop foresight.
Concept 3: The methodological status and legitimacy debate of futures studies (art vs. science, interdisciplinarity, and its relation to social sciences and history).

questions.question: Why do genres grow and change when audiences and creators change, and what does that tell us about the role of genre in organizing and interpreting art?

options.A: Genres are fixed templates set by institutions; they only change when those institutions rewrite the rules, so proliferation shows top-down control.
options.B: Genres are socially negotiated, flexible frameworks that adapt as audiences' expectations and creators' practices shift; this shows genre is a useful tool to interpret and organize new artistic work.
options.C: Genres are random marketing labels with little impact on interpretation; their growth just reflects publicity and commercial trends.
options.D: Genres are mathematical, fixed categories that should remain stable, so any proliferation contradicts proper classification of art.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, used clearer language, removed detailed historical examples but kept the core idea that genres adapt socially and help organize art.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Genre conventions are socially agreed-upon frameworks that guide what a genre is and how information is communicated; these conventions can evolve over time.
Concept 2: Genres are dynamic and proliferate in response to changes in audiences and creators, using genre as a tool to interpret and organize artistic output.

Concept 3: The historical/theoretical framework of genres (e.g., hierarchy of genres and its institutional backing) shapes how genres are evaluated, organized, and taught within art history and criticism.

questions.question: How do the middle ear ossicles help transfer sound from the air into the fluid of the cochlea, and which mechanical features do this?

options.A: They reduce energy loss by damping vibrations across all frequencies.
options.B: They convert airborne vibrations into neural signals in the middle ear using resonance of the tympanic membrane.
options.C: They overcome impedance mismatch by increasing pressure at the oval window using the tympanic membrane-to-stapes area ratio and the lever action of the malleus–incus.
options.D: They amplify only high-frequency sounds via selective resonance of the ossicles.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question one sentence, used simpler wording, kept technical terms (impedance mismatch, area ratio, lever action) and preserved the original correct mechanism.
concepts_tested: Outer ear function and localization: the pinna and ear canal focus/filter sound and provide vertical localization cues due to asymmetry.
Middle ear impedance matching: the ossicles (malleus, incus, stapes) overcome impedance mismatch between air and cochlear fluids to efficiently transmit vibrations.
Inner ear transduction: the organ of Corti in the cochlea converts mechanical vibrations into neural impulses via hair cells, enabling auditory perception.

questions.question: In the resource-based view, isolating mechanisms keep a firm's valuable resources from spreading to rivals. Why are these mechanisms important and how do they work?

options.A: They make resources spread faster among rivals, reducing firms' differences.
options.B: They make it hard for competitors to copy or obtain the firm's valuable resources, keeping the firm's unique resource mix over time.
options.C: They convert tacit knowledge into easy-to-follow rules, making imitation simple.
options.D: They rely only on legal ownership across partners to block all paths to resource superiority.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question and options, used simpler wording while keeping the idea that isolating mechanisms prevent rivals from accessing or copying valuable resources.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Resource heterogeneity across firms leads to different strategies and competitive positions.
Concept 2: Internal resources (assets, capabilities, and competencies) are the source of competitive advantage; firms should identify and leverage them.
Concept 3: Isolating mechanisms protect valuable resources from diffusion, helping sustain competitive advantage.

questions.question: Why does legitimacy (being seen as rightful) matter for a government's ability to act and have people follow its decisions?

options.A: Legitimacy is irrelevant; authority relies only on coercion and force.
options.B: Legitimacy gives a moral or legal reason to obey, so people comply more willingly and authority works beyond just force.
options.C: Legitimacy limits authority to symbolic duties and prevents it from taking meaningful action.
options.D: Legitimacy guarantees that all citizens will consent to every policy.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question one sentence, briefly defined 'legitimacy', shortened each option to one clear sentence while keeping the original meanings.
concepts_tested: Legitimacy as the basis for authority and its relation to the power to act
Institutional frameworks and jurisdiction (branches of government, sovereignty) as mechanisms that structure and limit authority
The relationship between authority, freedom, and political obligations (e.g., the idea of limited government)

questions.question: How does the interdependence of people, organizations, and information systems change how we design and use those systems?

options.A: By assuming technology alone will produce the best results, so design focuses only on technical performance and ignores users and workflows.
options.B: By treating users as people who must adapt to the system after it's built, leaving organizational routines unchanged.
options.C: By seeing users, organizational routines, and information flows as linked feedback loops, so design must address tasks, rules, and how data moves together.
options.D: By prioritizing security above all else and treating people and organizations only as constraints on system operation.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question and options, removed heavy jargon, kept core idea that design must consider users, organizations, and information flows together; preserved original correct answer and contrasts.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Systems-thinking and stakeholder-centered problem solving (tackling systemic problems rather than isolated technologies)
Concept 2: Transdisciplinary integration across technical, organizational, and human dimensions (bridging informatics, library science, HCI, ethics, etc.)
Concept 3: Interdependence of people, organizations, and information systems (how user needs, organizational contexts, and information flow shape design and use)

questions.question: The "front doors" principle says a site should give multiple entry points to content. How does offering multiple 'front doors' help users with different goals find what they need?

options.A: It forces everyone to use one main path, which supposedly reduces complexity.
options.B: It gives different entry points that match users' mental models and goals, so they can reach relevant content directly.
options.C: It makes all content accessible only through a single global search box.
options.D: It removes labels so users must click around randomly until they find content.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, replaced 'findability' with 'find what they need', and kept option meanings concise while keeping the correct answer unchanged.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Information architecture as the structural design that uses organization, labeling, search, and navigation to improve usability and findability in information environments.

Concept 2: The Principles of information architecture (objects, choices, disclosure, exemplars, front doors, multiple classification, focused navigation, growth) as a design framework guiding how information should be organized and accessed.

Concept 3: IA as an interdisciplinary, cross-domain framework linking design, architecture, and information science, and its role across web, information systems, data architecture, and enterprise contexts.

questions.question: Which statement best explains why collective memory keeps a group's perspective and causes different groups to remember different events?

options.A: Collective memory combines many perspectives to avoid bias, while history records a single group's narrative.
options.B: Collective memory centers on a group's shared values and stories, selecting and framing events to reinforce identity; history tries to include multiple perspectives, so different groups recall and interpret events differently.
options.C: History completely excludes values and bias, and collective memory is so biased that it cannot be studied scientifically.
options.D: Both use objective rules that should make memories identical across groups, so any differences are just random errors.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened wording, kept the contrast between collective memory (group-focused, selective, identity-building) and history (multi-perspective, objective aim); made options concise and plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Collective memory as a socially constructed, shared pool of memories tied to a social group's identity and transmitted within that group.

Concept 2: The difference between collective memory and history, including aims, perspectives, and biases (collective memory often reflects the values/narratives of a particular group rather than a multi-perspective, objective account).

Concept 3: The construction and transmission of collective memory through narratives, values, and group processes, and how this leads to cross-group (e.g., national or generational) differences in recollection.

questions.question: Why does making a broad master plan early in a landscape architecture project help ensure that built (hard) and planted (soft) systems work together later?

options.A: Because early master planning sets spatial relationships and performance goals that guide technical choices—aligning infrastructure, drainage, planting, and public spaces into a coherent system.
options.B: Because it removes all uncertainty so later design work isn't needed.
options.C: Because it only focuses on looks and leaves structural and technical decisions until later.
options.D: Because it prioritises short-term cost savings and ignores long-term ecological needs.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one clear sentence, defined 'built' and 'planted' in parentheses, and made each option concise while preserving the original meanings.
concepts_tested: Interdisciplinary integration: Landscape architecture combines urban design, ecology, engineering, horticulture, psychology, and more to achieve multiple outcomes.

Early concept/master planning as pivotal: The initial concept and master plan drive subsequent design, technical work, and implementation, highlighting a cause–effect relationship between early ideas and project outcomes.
Built/green system integration and sustainability: The discipline balances hard (built) and soft (planted) elements and emphasizes ecological sustainability in design decisions.

questions.question: How does including boundaries, setbacks, rights of way, utilities, and the placement of buildings, roads, and landscaping in a site plan affect the design process?

options.A: It lets designers explore looks without limits and delays checking rules until later.
options.B: It enables early multidisciplinary coordination so siting, circulation, and landscape decisions align with infrastructure and regulations, reducing later changes.
options.C: It replaces separate technical studies so no further analysis is needed after the plan is drawn.
options.D: It locks in one layout that cannot be changed if regulations or site conditions change.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, kept technical terms (setbacks, rights of way, utilities), and made all options concise and plausible while preserving the original correct choice.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Site analysis as a data-driven precursor that determines where and how development should occur (e.g., avoiding floodplains or steep slopes, leveraging favorable conditions).
Concept 2: The site plan as a map of relationships among built form, infrastructure, landscape elements, and regulatory constraints (boundaries, setbacks, rights of way) that shapes design decisions.
Concept 3: Multidisciplinary integration and representation (architectural, landscape, engineering, urban planning inputs) expressed through a scaled top-down drawing to coordinate improvements.

questions.question: In Mackinder's Heartland Theory, why would controlling the Eurasian interior give a state global power, and how does this idea differ from the logic of sea power?

options.A: Because control of the Heartland gives secure overland movement and access to resources that are less vulnerable to naval blockade, letting a state project power across continents; sea-power logic instead stresses controlling sea lanes and naval reach.
options.B: Because the interior is sparsely populated and easy to govern from far away, similar to how sea powers relied on distant coaling stations.
options.C: Because land power is always superior in every terrain, making maritime routes irrelevant to global strategy.
options.D: Because modern technology makes geography unimportant, so holding the Heartland has no strategic value.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the sentence, defined the contrast between Heartland (land-based control, resources, vulnerability to naval blockade) and sea power (control of sea lanes). Removed academic jargon and extra historical detail while keeping the core mechanism and contrast.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Spatial structures and political processes are interrelated; geography shapes political outcomes and politics shapes spatial organization.
Concept 2: Heartland Theory as a geopolitical framework: control of the Eurasian heartland yields global power, contrasting with sea-power paradigms.
Concept 3: Environmental determinism and Lebensraum as mechanisms linking the physical environment to state expansion and imperialist policy.

questions.question: In Marxist historical materialism, the economic base (how a society produces goods and the relations of production) shapes politics, law, and culture (the superstructure). How does the base produce these changes and what links them?

options.A: The superstructure drives economic change by creating cultural norms that alter how production is organised, so politics and ideas reshape the base.
options.B: The base's relations of production create class interests and power groups that control political institutions and spread ideologies, thereby shaping the superstructure.
options.C: The base and superstructure influence each other equally and independently, with no single dominant force linking them.
options.D: The base affects only technical tools and production methods; politics and culture develop independently, driven mainly by ideas.

questions.simplification_notes: Explained 'base' and 'superstructure' in parentheses, shortened wording to two sentences, kept technical terms (relations of production, class interests) and preserved the original correct answer.
concepts_tested: Historical materialism and the base–superstructure model as mechanisms linking economy to all other aspects of society
Class struggle and exploitation as engines of social conflict and change
Internal contradictions of capitalism leading to crises and a potential proletarian revolution toward socialism/communism

questions.question: Why does the "Pictorial Turn" say images, rather than words, become central to how we understand culture, and how does technology matter in that change?

options.A: It says images are always more truthful than texts, so culture should be read mainly through visuals.
options.B: It says images act as active mediators: they shape perception, produce meaning, and spread knowledge through visual technologies, changing how knowledge is made and shared.
options.C: It claims language stays the main source of meaning and images only mirror existing textual ideas.
options.D: It argues visual culture ignores social context and focuses only on aesthetics.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, removed dense jargon and examples, and kept the core idea: images become central and technology mediates visual experience.
concepts_tested: The mediation of visual experience by technology: how visual technologies alter perception, meaning, and the production/dissemination of images.
The Pictorial Turn / iconic turn as paradigm shifts: why images, rather than language, become central to understanding culture and knowledge.

Interdisciplinary scope and conceptual framework of visual culture: how visual culture connects with multiple fields and treats “visual events” as interfaces where information, meaning, or pleasure are produced.

questions.question: Why is the differential $df_{x_0}$ the best linear approximation to a differentiable function $f$ near the point $x_0$?

options.A: Because for small increments $h$, $f(x_0+h)=f(x_0)+df_{x_0}(h)+o(\|h\|)$, so $df_{x_0}$ matches the first-order change while higher-order terms vanish faster than $\|h\|$.
options.B: Because it minimizes the maximum error of any linear approximation on a fixed small neighborhood around $x_0$.
options.C: Because it is determined by second derivatives and so encodes the curvature of $f$ near $x_0$.
options.D: Because it is the exact inverse of the Jacobian matrix at $x_0$.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question one sentence, kept technical terms, and included the precise first-order formula in LaTeX; answer choices shortened and kept plausible distractors.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Linear equations, linear maps, and their representations in vector spaces and matrices are interconnected aspects of modeling linear relationships.
Concept 2: Linear algebra functions as a unifying framework linking algebra, geometry, and analysis (including its role in geometry and as a basis for function spaces in functional analysis).
Concept 3: Local linear approximation of nonlinear systems through the differential, i.e., the linear map that best approximates a function near a point.

questions.question: If popular sovereignty is a principle, not one fixed political form, how can a non-democratic government still claim it is legitimate?

options.A: It means every decision must be made by a direct vote of all citizens.
options.B: Because legitimacy rests on the consent of the governed, the exact system—democratic, monarchical, or other—can be justified by laws or constitutional rules that claim to express or produce that consent.
options.C: It removes all legal limits on rulers so any leader who claims popular support is automatically legitimate.
options.D: It ties legitimacy only to holding regular elections at fixed intervals.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened to one sentence; removed named philosophers and detailed history; options kept concise and plausible while preserving the core idea that a principle allows different implementations and consent can be claimed by legal or constitutional means.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Legitimacy derives from the consent of the governed; the source of political authority is the people.
Concept 2: Social contract as the mechanism by which individuals authorize governance and define limits on power (with distinctions among Hobbes, Locke, Rousseau and the idea of the general will).

Concept 3: Distinction between principle and implementation; popular sovereignty as a principle that does not dictate a specific political system and may coexist with non-democratic forms or legalistic interpretations.

questions.question: How do checks and balances prevent one branch of government from gaining too much power, and what is the main way they achieve this?

options.A: By giving each branch completely separate powers so no branch can affect the others, ensuring total independence.
options.B: By requiring inter-branch consent, vetoes, or oversight, so any attempt to expand power must overcome opposition and bargaining costs that deter unilateral grabs.
options.C: By placing final authority in a single supreme court that alone decides whether other branches’ actions are valid.
options.D: By removing separation and combining roles so decisions are faster but controlled by a single fused process.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, used clearer plain language, kept the original choices’ meanings but made each option concise and plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Distinction of state functions as a constitutional principle (legislative, executive, judicial) to preserve the integrity of each function and prevent conflation of powers.
Concept 2: Mechanisms of checks and balances (inter-branch independence, reciprocal constraints, and the idea of fusion vs. separation) to prevent the concentration or abuse of power.

Concept 3: Historical and theoretical foundations (Polybius, Locke, and the evolution toward a tripartite system) that explain why the model emerged and how it is applied in practice (e.g., early English instruments, development of judicial separation).

questions.question: How can a cell change what it does without changing its DNA, and why is RNA needed as an information middleman?

options.A: RNA is a tunable messenger: by controlling transcription and translation, cells change protein amounts quickly and reversibly without mutating DNA.
options.B: Only changing the DNA sequence can change cell behavior; RNA has no role in regulating proteins.
options.C: Proteins automatically change their function when the environment shifts, so RNA or DNA changes are unnecessary.
options.D: The DNA→RNA→protein pathway is a fixed one-way pipeline that cannot be regulated, so cells must alter DNA to change traits.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question one clear sentence, used simple terms (transcription/translation explained as control of protein amounts), shortened each option to one sentence, and kept the focus on reversible regulation versus DNA change.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Homeostasis and systemic regulation
Concept 2: Central dogma and protein-mediated cell function
Concept 3: DNA damage and repair leading to mutations

questions.question: How does evidence-based medicine (EBM) deal with differences in research strength while guiding care for an individual patient?

options.A: It applies the same rigid standard to all interventions and ignores context.
options.B: It uses a graded hierarchy of evidence and combines that with patient values and clinical context to tailor decisions.
options.C: It assumes all high-quality evidence applies directly to every patient without change.
options.D: It replaces clinician judgment with a fixed protocol that must be followed in all cases.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one clear sentence, defined EBM acronym, and made all options concise while keeping the original meaning.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Medicine as a synthesis of art (clinical skill, creativity) and science (biomedical knowledge, cellular/molecular understanding) and how they inform practice.
Concept 2: Evidence-based medicine as a framework guiding interventions, with acknowledged variability in evidence and its influence on clinical practice.
Concept 3: The relationship between traditional/folk medicine and modern scientific medicine, including issues of efficacy, safety, and ethical considerations (and the boundary with quackery).

questions.question: Researchers test whether fertilizer concentration affects plant growth by using identical plants and keeping water, light, soil, and age the same across groups. Why is this control important, and what risk arises if any of those other factors differ between groups?

options.A: It lets researchers attribute growth differences to the fertilizer because other factors are held constant; if another factor varied, that factor could be responsible (a confounder).
options.B: It makes the experiment look more professional, which increases the chance of getting published.
options.C: It guarantees no difference in growth will be seen, since all variables are the same across groups.
options.D: It removes the need to repeat the experiment, because constant conditions give a definitive result.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened and clarified the original scenario and question into one sentence; kept technical terms like 'confounder' with simple context; made all answer choices concise and plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Cause-and-effect through variable manipulation and controls (why/how manipulating an independent variable with controls isolates its effect).
Concept 2: The role of experiments in testing hypotheses within the scientific method (why/how experiments can support or disprove hypotheses and why they cannot prove them).
Concept 3: Variability in experimental design (natural vs. highly controlled experiments) and the importance of repeatability and logical analysis for reliability.

questions.question: Why does biased sampling weaken an inductive generalization from a sample to a population?

options.A: Because biased sampling increases the sample size, making the estimate more precise.
options.B: Because biased sampling creates systematic differences between the sample and the population, so the sample statistic may not reflect the true population parameter.
options.C: Because biased sampling makes the sample more representative of the population.
options.D: Because biased sampling only affects deductive conclusions, not inductive ones.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened to one sentence and wording made direct; options kept concise and plausible while preserving the original correct choice and core idea about representativeness and reliability.
concepts_tested: Inductive generalization: inferring population attributes from sample data; strength depends on sample size and representativeness.
Probabilistic inference mechanisms: Bayesian updating and maximum likelihood estimation as methods to quantify uncertainty and identify the most likely population distribution given observed data.
Fallacies and limitations: hasty generalization and biased sampling as factors that weaken or invalidate inductive conclusions.

questions.question: How does coordinating policy and rules across national, regional, and local levels help scale up health programs over time?

options.A: By putting all decisions at the national level so standards are the same everywhere.
options.B: By aligning policy content, operational rules, funding, and implementation capacity across national, regional, and local levels over time to support gradual and sustainable scale-up.
options.C: By using results from a single pilot setting alone to justify rapid expansion.
options.D: By preventing local adaptation so the original program stays exactly the same everywhere.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened and 'multi-level governance' explained as national/regional/local; kept the idea that alignment of policy content, rules, funding, and capacity over time enables sustainable scale-up.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Policy process and translation from national laws/policies to programs and services (policy content → operational rules → implementation → health outcomes)
Concept 2: Evidence-based policymaking (use of science and trials to identify effective programs and inform policy decisions; interplay with philosophical/economic debates)
Concept 3: Policy environment and multi-level governance for scalable health interventions (how governance at multiple levels and over time facilitates sustainable scale-up)

questions.question: By Noether's theorem, why does a differentiable symmetry under time translations give a conserved quantity in a Lagrangian system? If the Lagrangian $L(q,\dot q,t)$ has no explicit $t$ dependence, what is conserved?

options.A: The symmetry makes $L$ change only by a total time derivative so the action is unchanged; the conserved quantity is the Lagrangian $L$ itself.
options.B: The symmetry makes the action invariant under an infinitesimal time shift, giving a conserved Noether charge; for time translations this charge is the total energy (the Hamiltonian) $E=\sum_i p_i\dot q_i- L$.
options.C: The symmetry forces the momentum conjugate to time to be constant; the conserved quantity is this temporal conjugate momentum.
options.D: The symmetry removes dependence on potential energy, which forces the total energy to be zero.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened to two clear sentences, LaTeX used for $L(q,\dot q,t)$ and energy formula, options kept concise and plausible, correct answer unchanged.
concepts_tested: Local conservation via the continuity equation: how the change of a conserved quantity in a region equals the net flow (transport) of that quantity across the region’s boundary.

Noether’s theorem and symmetry-conservation relationship: why differentiable symmetries imply conservation laws (e.g., time translation symmetry → energy conservation; spatial isotropy → angular momentum conservation).
Exact vs. approximate conservation laws: how some laws hold universally while others apply only to certain processes or regimes, and how this distinction reflects underlying principles or symmetries.

questions.question: Why does turning a continuous system into a discrete numerical model let researchers study complex phenomena? How do choices like discretization resolution and time step affect the results?

options.A: Discretization turns the governing equations into algebraic problems that are solved exactly, independent of resolution.
options.B: Discretization converts continuous equations into a finite set of approximate equations that computers can solve; resolution and time stepping control accuracy, stability, and cost, so there is a trade-off between fidelity and compute time.
options.C: Discretization removes any ill-posedness and guarantees a unique solution even when the original continuous problem had none.
options.D: Discretization makes simulations faster and introduces no error because modern processors compute with perfect precision.

questions.simplification_notes: Split the original question into two clear sentences, used plain wording, kept technical terms (resolution, time step) and shortened answer choices to be concise and plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Computational science uses mathematical models and computer simulations to solve complex problems, complementing theory and experiments.
Concept 2: The methodology involves defining a system, designing data-extracting experiments, and using models to answer questions about the system.
Concept 3: The field relies on numerical analysis, computational mathematics, and high-performance computing to perform large-scale calculations (often on supercomputers).

questions.question: How do checks and balances limit power and promote accountability between branches of government?

options.A: They require every policy decision to be approved by all branches at the same time, forcing unanimous consent.
options.B: They divide powers so each branch can review, limit, or override others, creating interdependence and accountability.
options.C: They give all decision power to one branch while the others only advise afterwards.
options.D: They allow the executive to act quickly without oversight to ensure efficient government.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened to one sentence; answer choices made concise and clearer while keeping the original meanings and distinctions.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Functional differentiation of state power (legislative, executive, judicial/administrative) and the need for clear allocation to prevent fusion and preserve the integrity of each function.
Concept 2: Mechanisms of checks and balances and structural independence that constrain power and enable accountability among branches.
Concept 3: Variants and evolution of the model (triangular/political system, unity/fusion vs. separation, historical development) showing how the principle is implemented differently across contexts.

questions.question: During an outbreak, what changes when health authorities switch from passive surveillance to active surveillance, and why is active surveillance often chosen?

options.A: Active surveillance cuts costs and speeds reporting but reduces how complete the data are.
options.B: Passive surveillance is proactive case-finding with record reviews, so it improves timeliness but is more expensive.
options.C: Active surveillance uses proactive case-finding (visiting facilities and reviewing records), which improves timeliness and completeness but needs more resources, so it is used for epidemics or elimination efforts.
options.D: Passive surveillance automatically finds all cases through routine reports, ensuring maximum completeness and the fastest response.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened to one sentence; kept terms 'passive' and 'active surveillance' and the key trade-offs (timeliness, completeness, resources). Included short example activities for active surveillance.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The surveillance cycle and its role in informing public health action (data collection → analysis → interpretation → planning/implementation/evaluation → feedback).
Concept 2: Passive vs. active surveillance and the trade-offs/appropriate contexts for each (cost, time, completeness, and epidemic response).

Concept 3: The integration of diverse data sources and informatics (cancer/disease registries, syndromic surveillance, automated adverse event reporting) and how these support detection, monitoring, and policy decisions.

questions.question: If inputs and the final product stay the same, how does performing a primary activity more efficiently affect costs and profits?

options.A: It lowers the cost of that activity (cost per unit) by being more efficient, cutting total costs and raising profit if price is unchanged.
options.B: It changes the product's inherent value to customers, letting the firm charge a higher selling price.
options.C: It alters the legal order of steps in production, which changes who pays for the inputs.
options.D: It makes earlier activities irrelevant to the final price because the final step dominates value.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question shorter and clearer for high school students, kept the term 'primary activity', and shortened each option to one sentence while preserving the original meaning.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Value is created through a chain of activities that add value at each step, with cumulative value arising from the sequence.
Concept 2: The value chain is a system with inputs, transformation processes, and outputs; how activities are performed determines costs and profits.

Concept 3: Competitive advantage can be achieved by optimizing any of the primary activities (inbound logistics, operations, outbound logistics, marketing and sales, service), and the appropriate analytical level is the business unit rather than the entire company.

questions.question: How did moving stories from oral telling to written and digital forms change how they evolve across cultures, and why did this allow lasting cycles and motifs to form?

options.A: It removed variation by fixing a single canonical version, preventing cycles and motifs from forming.
options.B: It preserved local performance variation, while fixed, portable records let motifs spread and recombine across regions, enabling durable cycles and epic traditions.
options.C: It slowed transmission so stories depended on one storyteller, making cross-cultural motifs rare.
options.D: It reduced social interaction, causing stories to lose their educational and cultural guidance role.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, removed academic jargon, and kept answer choices concise while keeping the original idea about media change enabling spread and recombination of motifs.
concepts_tested: Storytelling as a social-cultural mechanism for entertainment, education, cultural preservation, and moral instruction
Core narrative elements (plot, characters, narrative point of view) as structural features enabling meaning-making
Transmission and evolution of storytelling across media and cultures (oral → written → diverse media; memetic spread; formation of cycles, motifs, and epic traditions)

questions.question: What does "disinterested pleasure" mean in judging art, and how does it try to connect a personal experience to a claim about an artwork's value?

options.A: It says we should appreciate an artwork for its formal features, apart from personal wants or practical concerns, so different observers can compare judgments.
options.B: It requires that only credentialed experts' opinions count as objective aesthetic judgments.
options.C: It claims aesthetic value depends only on how intense your emotions are, so mood decides the value.
options.D: It says a judgment is valid only if it matches the artist's stated intent.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened to two clear sentences; technical phrase 'disinterested pleasure' explained as impartial appreciation; options made concise and kept plausible alternatives.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The objectivity vs subjectivity of aesthetic properties and judgments, including the idea of disinterested pleasure in aesthetic experience.
Concept 2: The relationship between artworks and interpretation/criticism—how meaning, emotion, and authorial intent are identified and analyzed.
Concept 3: The interdisciplinary and cross-cultural relationships of aesthetics—how aesthetics relates to ethics, religion, psychology, and comparative traditions.

questions.question: In a hierarchical type system, what mainly prevents a construction like 'the set of all sets that do not contain themselves' (Russell's paradox)?

options.A: Elements can only belong to collections of a strictly higher type, so no collection can contain itself and self-reference is blocked.
options.B: All collections must be finite, so the universal self-containing collection required for the paradox cannot exist.
options.C: Self-referential definitions are banned so predicates cannot refer to their own truth value, preventing such sets.
options.D: There is one universal type that contains everything, which stops forming a separate 'set of all sets'.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence; used plain language; kept the core idea that a type hierarchy blocks self-membership; made all options concise and plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Hierarchical typing as a mechanism to avoid paradoxes (e.g., Russell's paradox) by restricting how entities of different types are constructed and preventing self-reference.
Concept 2: Type theory as an alternative foundation for mathematics and its relationships to set theory and constructive mathematics (e.g., intuitionistic type theory) as a theoretical framework.
Concept 3: The use of type theory in computational proof systems (e.g., calculus of constructions, Coq, Lean) to encode mathematics and support formal verification.

questions.question: Why must political goals shape how military forces are organized and used in strategic planning?

options.A: Political goals do not limit military actions; battlefield tactics can ignore politics.
options.B: One military plan fits every political aim, so strategies need not be changed.
options.C: Political aims decide what resources to mobilize, what risks to accept, and what trade-offs to make; if aims exceed available means, the strategy must be adjusted.
options.D: Strategy only depends on technical battlefield superiority and not on political objectives.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question one sentence, used clearer everyday wording, and shortened each option to one concise sentence while keeping the original meaning and correct answer.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Strategy aligns military means with political ends; political aims shape how military resources are planned and employed.
Concept 2: Hierarchy of warfare planning—tactics (on the battlefield), strategy (planning and conduct of campaigns), and grand strategy (nation-wide or coalition-level aims and resources).

Concept 3: The role of resources and the opponent’s will to fight in determining strategic outcomes (using resources to gain supremacy or reduce will to fight; influence of deception/asymmetry in strategy).

questions.question: Why does combining the physical design of a public space with its management and programming change how different people use and experience that space?

options.A: Because management alone decides how people use a space, regardless of how it is built.
options.B: Because design creates affordances (what the space allows people to do) and management signals social norms; together they shape activities, who feels welcome, and how safe the place seems.
options.C: Because attractive looks alone draw everyone equally, so programming or management is not needed.
options.D: Because only economic incentives determine use; design and management have little effect on people’s experience.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, clarified the term 'affordances' in option B, and kept the key idea that physical design plus management together shape use and experience; correct answer unchanged.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Public space as the central focus of urban design, and the relationship between design/management of spaces and how they are used and experienced.
Concept 2: Interdisciplinary integration across architecture, landscape architecture, urban planning, civil engineering, sociology, law, economics, etc., to produce holistic urban design outcomes.
Concept 3: Design objectives (equitable, beautiful, performative, sustainable) that guide decisions and define success in urban design.

questions.question: Why does a logical match between a sponsor and the sponsoree (the event or group being sponsored) boost sponsorship effectiveness more than just being seen?

options.A: It forms a stronger mental link so thinking of the event brings the brand to mind and gives better recall and more positive impressions.
options.B: It guarantees higher sales because the sponsorship communicates specific product features directly.
options.C: It removes the need for other marketing, since sponsorship alone will achieve all outcomes.
options.D: It lets the sponsor skip explaining why they support the event to the audience.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened wording to one sentence, defined 'sponsoree', and kept the key idea about mental links and memory associations.
concepts_tested: Concept 1
Concept 2
Concept 3

questions.question: How can a strictly programmed computer still be creative by breaking or bending rules?

options.A: Because deviating from rules makes outputs random, and random outputs are creative.
options.B: Because the system can explore beyond constraints using randomness, remixing, or recombination to produce novel and useful artifacts, and an evaluation step selects what is valuable.
options.C: Because only conscious intention can break rules meaningfully, so machines cannot be truly creative.
options.D: Because creativity is just rule-breaking, so any deviation from rules is automatically creative.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question shorter and clearer for high school readers; kept key terms (randomness, remixing, recombination, evaluation) but used simpler phrasing; answer choices shortened to 1–2 sentences.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Criteria for computational creativity (e.g., novelty, usefulness, value) and the existence of multiple frameworks (Newell-Shaw-Simon vs. Boden) that operationalize creativity.
Concept 2: The philosophical/mechanistic question of whether machines can be creative if they are constrained by programming, including the role of rule-breaking or deviation from convention.
Concept 3: The relationship between theoretical inquiry and practical system design in computational creativity—how theory informs implementations and practical work, and vice versa.

questions.question: How did GPU architectures speed up progress in deep learning, and what principle explains why that mattered for neural networks?

options.A: They increased single-thread CPU clock speeds, enabling faster serial computation.
options.B: They provide massive data-level parallelism ideal for dense linear algebra (matrix multiplications) that underpins neural network training, making training on large datasets feasible.
options.C: They introduced new specialized training algorithms that run only on GPUs, replacing backpropagation.
options.D: They dramatically reduced memory usage so much larger networks fit into the same hardware without adding memory.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question one sentence, kept technical terms like 'data-level parallelism' and 'matrix multiplications', shortened option texts and removed broader historical/ethical context while retaining the core hardware→algorithm causal idea.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The relationship between AI goals (learning, reasoning, perception, planning) and the techniques used to achieve them (search, optimization, formal logic, neural networks, statistics). How do specific goals drive the choice of methods, and how do different subfields align with particular tools?

Concept 2: The causal role of hardware and architectural advances in AI progress (GPUs enabling deep learning; transformer architecture enabling generative AI; the AI boom after 2020). Why/how did progress accelerate at these points, and what roles do hardware vs. algorithms play?

Concept 3: The emergence of ethical concerns and regulatory considerations in response to AI capabilities and potential harms (long-term effects, existential risks, safety policies). Why/how do capabilities lead to policy discussions, and how might regulation influence future development?

questions.question: How do predefined performance indicators make units comparable, and what is the main trade-off they cause?

options.A: They guarantee universally accurate measurements so local differences don't matter.
options.B: They set identical targets for all units, enforcing fairness but reducing flexibility for local conditions.
options.C: They create a common measurement framework for comparing units but can miss local constraints and priorities, trading contextual relevance for standardization.
options.D: They remove subjectivity and make assessments fully objective and independent of context.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened and clarified the question; kept the core idea that standard indicators aid comparison but reduce context-specific relevance; options made concise and plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The purpose-driven nature of performance measurement. Why the objective of measuring (forward-looking, retrospective, evaluative) changes what gets measured and how results are used.
Concept 2: Standards vs. context in performance measurement. How predefined indicators enable comparability but may constrain context-specific assessment, and the trade-offs involved.

Concept 3: Frameworks and alignment of measures with performance. How organizing frameworks integrate indicators across an organization and link measurement to overall performance, including the reality of divergent viewpoints on how to define/use measures.

questions.question: A sealed box contains $N$ identical molecules that follow time-symmetric Newtonian mechanics. If the gas starts in a low-entropy state and entropy then increases, why does macroscopic irreversibility appear even though the microscopic laws are time-reversible?

options.A: Microscopic interactions create real dissipative forces that pick a time direction, so reverse evolution is impossible.
options.B: High-entropy macrostates correspond to astronomically many more microstates, so almost any evolution from a typical low-entropy microstate will go toward higher entropy; the exact reversed microstate is extraordinarily unlikely.
options.C: Measurement or observation collapses the system into a single history, producing an apparent arrow of time.
options.D: Gravity or the potential energy makes the system minimize energy only in forward time, forcing entropy to rise.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened and clarified the scenario using $N$ for molecule count; preserved the statistical entropy explanation as the correct answer; removed technical examples and extra context.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Time-reversal symmetry vs macroscopic irreversibility; how microscopic laws can be time-symmetric while macroscopic processes are not, due to entropy production.
Concept 2: Entropy and the second law as the statistical mechanism behind the arrow of time; increasing entropy corresponds to decreasing free energy and a preferred temporal direction.

Concept 3: The role of initial/boundary conditions (e.g., low-entropy Big Bang) and cosmological context in establishing or constraining the arrow of time, including how global conditions influence local time asymmetry.

questions.question: Why is risk management the central idea in aviation safety, and how does it work to lower risk in research, training, design, and infrastructure?

options.A: It tries to remove every hazard so no accidents can ever happen.
options.B: It depends only on following regulations, assuming rules make risk zero.
options.C: It is a continuous, evidence-based process: identify hazards, assess risks, apply layered protections across people, procedures, and systems, and monitor results to adapt, reducing both the chance and severity of accidents.
options.D: It focuses on responding after incidents happen rather than preventing them.

questions.simplification_notes: Wording shortened to one clear question; kept the core idea of continuous hazard identification, assessment, layered mitigations across people/procedures/systems, and monitoring; removed long examples and background details.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Risk management as the core principle of aviation safety (reducing risk through research, training, design, and infrastructure)
Concept 2: The role of regulation and oversight as mechanisms to reduce risk across all aspects of flight
Concept 3: Relationships between environmental factors (e.g., adverse weather) and safety outcomes, and the distinction between safety and security

questions.question: Why does copyright protect the original expression of an idea, but not the idea itself?

options.A: Because protecting ideas would stop others from creating new works since ideas are universal and should be free to use.
options.B: Because ideas are intangible and can't be fixed in a form, while expressions are fixed and uniquely created.
options.C: Because copyright gives exclusive rights to the author's particular expression, while the underlying idea stays available for others to use in new forms.
options.D: Because once a work exists, the idea it contains automatically becomes public domain and cannot be protected.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened and phrasing simplified; legal terms reduced while keeping the distinction between idea and expression and the role of exclusive rights. All answer choices kept plausible and concise.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Copyright protects the original expression of an idea, not the idea itself (principle driving what is protected).

Concept 2: Exclusive rights granted to rights holders (reproduction, distribution, derivative works, public performance, moral rights) and the role of limitations (e.g., fair use/fair dealing) as mechanisms balancing protection with public interest.
Concept 3: Territorial nature and duration of rights (copyright as a territorial right; cross-border implications; eventual entry into the public domain).

questions.question: Why does cohesion being multidimensional and dynamic matter for how a group's cohesion changes from formation to disbanding (e.g., social relations, task relations, perceived unity, emotions)?

options.A: Because only emotional satisfaction drives members to stay, so other dimensions can be ignored over time.
options.B: Because cohesion comes from several linked dimensions (social relations, task relations, perceived unity, emotions); strengths in some can temporarily cover weaknesses in others, but gradual declines across many dimensions will erode overall cohesion as the group evolves.
options.C: Because once cohesion is established it stays fixed across all dimensions, regardless of time or task changes.
options.D: Because social attraction alone determines future cohesion, making task relations unimportant.

questions.simplification_notes: Question and options were shortened, technical phrases were simplified, and the four main cohesion dimensions were listed; sentences kept direct and concise while preserving the original meaning.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Cohesion is multidimensional and dynamic (social relations, task relations, perceived unity, emotions; cohesion evolves over time from group formation to disbandment).

Concept 2: Antecedents and mechanisms of cohesion (attraction, coordination, sense of belonging, shared emotions), including distinctions such as interpersonal attraction vs. social/group-level attraction.

Concept 3: Instrumental basis and emotional dimension of cohesion and their link to outcomes (coherence for task or social purposes; emotional satisfaction leading to greater participation and persistence).

questions.question: How does the Matthew effect explain why power gaps between social groups with different starting resources tend to persist and grow?

options.A: Groups with early resource advantages gain access to influential networks and institutions, creating feedback loops that increase their resources and power.
options.B: Social systems automatically redistribute resources fairly, reducing initial gaps and equalizing power.
options.C: Resource-rich groups just hoard assets without changing networks or influence, so power stays the same over time.
options.D: Random large shocks (like crashes or windfalls) redistribute resources unpredictably, so gaps are as likely to shrink as to widen regardless of starting resources.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, used clearer language, kept key idea of cumulative advantage (feedback loops) and made distractors plausible and concise.
concepts_tested: Resource competition and power struggles between social groups
Economic structure (mode of production/base-superstructure) shaping social life and consciousness
Cumulative advantage: resources empower some groups to maintain or increase power (Matthew effect)

questions.question: How does a coherent business model narrative (a 'recipe') influence what entrepreneurs do, beyond just describing the business?

options.A: It serves as a clear blueprint that turns the value-creation logic into shared rules and signals, guiding decisions and coordinating actions across the firm and its partners.
options.B: It is just storytelling and has no real effect on how resources are allocated or what choices are made.
options.C: It guarantees fast growth and profit if the story is consistent, regardless of market conditions.
options.D: It removes the need to test assumptions because formal contracts and legal structures can replace experimentation.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, explained 'coherent narrative' as a 'recipe' or 'consistent story', and made all options concise while keeping the original correct choice.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The function of a business model as the design for creating, delivering, and capturing value to generate profit.
Concept 2: Business model innovation as a mechanism for adapting to opportunities and aligning with business strategy.
Concept 3: The role of narrative/coherence (and the idea of model “recipes”) as mechanisms that communicate the business logic and guide entrepreneurial action.

questions.question: Two games have the same core rules but different feedback timing: one gives immediate, clear feedback for each action and the other gives delayed, subtle feedback. What does this difference best show about the link between mechanics (rules), gameplay (player actions), and player experience (how players feel)?

options.A: Immediate feedback changes the actual rules, so it creates different gameplay and a different player experience.
options.B: Delayed feedback uncovers hidden rules that weren't in the game, effectively adding new gameplay.
options.C: Feedback timing changes how players interact with the same rules (gameplay) and how they feel (experience) without altering the core mechanics.
options.D: Feedback timing only affects the game's look and feel (aesthetics) and does not change gameplay or experience.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened to one sentence, technical terms kept with short parenthetical clarifications; answer choices made concise and kept plausible distractors.
concepts_tested: The relationship between game mechanics/systems, gameplay, and player experience, and how each influences the others.
The iterative design process, including prototyping and playtesting, as a mechanism for refining rules and mechanics.
The division of design/development roles (designer, developer, artist) and how their collaboration translates a concept into a final game.

questions.question: Why can anatomically homologous structures be seen as related in different species even when they now do different jobs?

options.A: Convergent evolution: unrelated lineages independently evolved similar-looking structures under similar selective pressures.
options.B: Shared ancestry: the species inherited the same structural blueprint, which was modified by divergent evolution for different functions.
options.C: Parallel mutation rates in separate lineages produce identical structures independently.
options.D: Random fixation of identical mutations in both lineages always creates identical structures.
